claim: After homogenization, the steels were hot rolled with a 40% thickness reduction and water quenched, then cold rolled with an 80% thickness reduction.
evidence_text: hot-rolling with a thickness reduction of 40 %, followed by water quenching. ... Then, hot-rolled plates were subjected to cold rolling process with an 80 % reduction.

claim: Uniaxial tensile testing was performed on flat dog-bone samples with a gauge length of 14 mm, width of 4 mm along the rolling direction, at a strain rate of 10^{-3} s^{-1} using an Instron 5892 with a contact extensometer.
evidence_text: flat dog-bone-shaped samples with a gauge length of 14 mm and width of 4 mm were prepared from the cold-rolling plate along rolling direction (RD). The uniaxial tensile testing was conducted using Instron 5892, and the experimental data were recorded via a contact extensometer at a strain rate of $1 0 ^ { - 3 }$ $s ^ { - 1 }$.

claim: Quasi-in-situ tensile testing with EBSD and μ-DIC was performed; a nanoscale SiO2 particle monolayer was used for patterning, and SEM images were captured at global strains of 0%, 2%, and 8% and analyzed with VIC-2D software to map local strain fields.
evidence_text: To reveal the local strain evolution ... a monolayer of nanoscale SiO2 particles was dispersed on the polished sample surface. The sample was then subjected to a quasi in-situ tensile deformation ... These images were taken at the global strain level of 0 %, 2 % and 8 %, respectively, and then were analyzed using VIC-2D software to obtain local strain fields at different strain levels.

claim: In the Fe-25Mn-7Al-0.9C-0.50Zr steel, a single-phase bimodal grain structure is obtained, consisting of soft coarse grains ~5 μm in size (65% area) and hard fine grains ~1 μm in size (35% area).
evidence_text: As Zr content increases to 0.50 wt %, a single-phase bimodal grain structure is successfully engineered, including soft coarse grain zones (~5 μm in size and 65 % in area) and hard fine grain zones (~1 μm in size and 35 % in area).

claim: Quasi-in-situ tensile testing with EBSD and μ-DIC was conducted on a sample surface prepared by mechanical grinding and polishing, then dispersed with a monolayer of nanoscale SiO2 particles; SEM images were captured at global strains of 0%, 2%, and 8% and analyzed with VIC-2D to obtain local strain fields.
evidence_text: the sample surface was prepared by mechanical grinding and polishing. Then, a monolayer of nanoscale SiO2 particles was dispersed on the polished sample surface. The sample was then subjected to a quasi in-situ tensile deformation using the Instron 5892 universal testing machine. Further, the deformed sample surface with nanoscale SiO2 was captured using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi SU7000). These images were taken at the global strain level of 0 %, 2 % and 8 %, respectively, and then were analyzed using VIC-2D software to obtain local strain fields at different strain levels.

claim: The Zr0.50 steel exhibits a heterogeneous feature with coarse FCC grains (CG) and fine FCC grains (FG), and enlarged BSE reveals a high density of uniformly distributed fine FCC particles in the FG regions.
evidence_text: the microstructure of the Zr0.50 steel exhibits a typical heterogeneous feature, i. e., coarse FCC grains (CG) and fine FCC grains (FG) ... An enlarged BSE image (Fig. 1c1) clearly reveals that a high density of fine FCC particles is uniformly distributed in the FG regions.

claim: EBSD IPF, IQ, and KAM maps of the Zr0 steel show equiaxed FCC grains with low KAM values, indicating low intragranular misorientation.
evidence_text: the EBSD data including inverse pole figure (IPF), image quality (IQ) and kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps (Fig. 2a–a2) reveal that its microstructure has equiaxed FCC grains with low KAM values.

claim: With the addition of 0.01 wt% Zr, the average size of recrystallized FCC grains is obviously reduced and the grain orientations are random.
evidence_text: Further adding micro-alloyed Zr in the Fe-Mn-Al-C low density steel, the average size of recrystallized FCC grains is obviously reduced and their grain orientations are also random, as shown in Fig. 2b–b2.

claim: EBSD analysis of Zr0.50 steel reveals a distinct heterostructure with coarse grain (CG) zones showing low KAM and fine grain (FG) zones exhibiting high KAM, indicating a high density of dislocations in the fine grains.
evidence_text: Fig. 2c–c2 reveals that the microstructure exhibits a distinct heterostructure including CG zones and FG zones. Compared to the CG zones with low KAM values, the FG zones exhibit a high KAM value, indicating a high density of dislocations are decorated in these fine FCC grains.

claim: With increasing Zr content from 0 to 0.50 wt%, yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and fracture ductility increase: Zr0 (YS ~435 MPa, UTS 820 MPa, ductility 52%), Zr0.01 (YS ~450 MPa, UTS ~832 MPa, ductility 55%), Zr0.50 (YS ~505 MPa, UTS ~855 MPa, ductility 58%).
evidence_text: In Zr0 steel, its yield strength is ~435 MPa, the ultimate tensile strength is 820 MPa, and fracture ductility is 52 %. As Zr content increased to 0.01 wt %, the strengths of the Zr0.01 steel are slightly increased, i.e., yield strength: ~450 MPa, ultimate tensile strength: ~832 MPa, while its fracture ductility is increased to 55 %. With further increasing of Zr content, the Zr0.50 steel exhibits a reasonable strength-ductility combination, i.e., yield strength: ~505 MPa, ultimate tensile strength: ~855 MPa and fracture ductility: 58 %.

claim: In the heterogeneous Zr0.50 steel, at 2% global strain, strain concentrates in the soft coarse grain (CG) zones and at CG/FG interfaces, while hard fine grain (FG) zones remain nearly undeformed; at 8% strain, strain in FG zones becomes more pronounced. This strain partitioning generates geometrically necessary dislocations, promoting heterogeneous deformation-induced (HDI) strengthening.
evidence_text: significant strain partitioning is observed at a minor plastic strain of 2 %. The CG zones and hetero-interfaces between FG and CG zones experiences high strain, whereas the FG zones remain nearly undeformed. This is mainly attributed to the hardness differences between CG zones (soft regions) and FG zones (hard regions). As the global strain increased to 8 % (Fig. 5c), the local strain of the FG zones becomes more pronounced, indicating that the strain partitioning between soft and hard regions persists during plastic deformation. Such strain partitioning behavior leads to the generation of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs), promoting a heterogeneous deformation-induced (HDI) strengthening.

claim: In Zr0.50 steel, planar dislocation slip is activated in the FCC matrix at minor plastic strain, and after severe deformation a high density of slip bands along {111} planes is observed, contributing to strengthening.
evidence_text: dislocations as a vital deformation mechanism are initially activated in FCC matrix during a minor of plastic deformation. At the fracture surface, the severe deformed FCC matrix (Fig. 6b) exhibits a high density of slip bands along 111planes, which generally leads to a strengthening effect.

claim: Nanotwins approximately 20 nm in size form during heavy deformation in Zr0.50 steel, surrounded by high-density dislocations; the interaction between dislocations and nanotwins reduces mean free path of mobile dislocations, leading to twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP). HRTEM reveals multiple stacking faults at twin boundaries, indicating the transformation from Shockley partial dislocations to twins.
evidence_text: nanotwins with ~20 nm in size are generated during a heavy deformation ... In the meantime, a high density of dislocation is decorated around the nanotwinned region, which contributes to the interaction between dislocations and nanotwins. Such behavior further effectively reduces the mean free paths of mobile dislocations, thereby leading to a typical twinning-induced plasticity. Fig. 6c1 reveals that twin boundaries are decorated by multiple stacking faults, which indicates that the nanotwins formation originates from the transition from multiple Shockley partial dislocations to twins.

claim: Numerous nanosized particles approximately 30 nm in size, identified as ZrC carbides by HRTEM and FFT, are distributed in the FCC grain interior of the deformed Zr0.50 steel, effectively inhibiting dislocation motion and providing precipitation strengthening.
evidence_text: Fig. 6d shows that numerous particles with ~30 nm in size are distributed in FCC grain interior, which can effectively inhibit the movement of dislocations, i.e., precipitation strengthening. A detail analysis on these nanoscale particles via HRTEM and corresponding FFT pattern (Fig. 6d1) confirms that these nanosized particles are identified as ZrC carbides.

claim: Numerous particles with ~30 nm in size are distributed in FCC grain interior, providing precipitation strengthening, and are identified as ZrC carbides by HRTEM and FFT pattern (Fig. 6d).
evidence_text: Fig. 6d shows that numerous particles with ~30 nm in size are distributed in FCC grain interior, which can effectively inhibit the movement of dislocations, i.e., precipitation strengthening. A detail analysis on these nanoscale particles via HRTEM and corresponding FFT pattern (Fig. 6d) confirms that these nanosized particles are identified as ZrC carbides, due to the addition of Zr and the strong affinity between Zr and C [20].

claim: Nanoscale ZrC particles with a size of ~30 nm inhibit grain boundary migration during recrystallization through a Zener pinning effect, contributing to partial recrystallization.
evidence_text: These nanoscale ZrC particles also inhibit the migration of grain boundaries during recrystallization through a typical Zener pinning effect. Compared to the Zr0 and Zr0.01 steels, the fraction of ZrC carbides in Zr0.50 steel is highest. Based on Fig. 6d, the size of ZrC in the Zr0.50 steel is only 30 nm. As a result, these nanoscale ZrC carbides in the Zr0.50 steel can lead to a desirable Zener pinning force, thereby playing a pivotal role of the partial recrystallization.

claim: Thermodynamic calculation (Fig. 7) shows that the fraction of ZrC particles is ~0.6% after annealing at 900 °C in Zr0.50 steel.
evidence_text: according to the thermodynamic calculation (Fig. 7), the fraction of ZrC particles is ~0.6 % after annealing at 900 °C.

claim: Micro shear bands caused by heavy cold-rolling are the preferred sites for the nucleation of recrystallization, providing sufficient driving force for fine grain formation.
evidence_text: the micro shear bands caused by the heavy cold-rolling deformation are the preferred sites for the nucleation of recrystallization [24,25], which provides the enough driving force to formation of fine grains.

claim: The fraction of coarse grains adjacent to fine grains, f_CG,b, is ~37% based on EBSD result (Fig. 2), used in HDI strengthening calculation.
evidence_text: f_CG,b is the fraction of those CGs adjacent to FGs, which is ~37 % based on EBSD result (Fig. 2).

claim: Based on Fig. 3, the width of hetero-boundary affected region l_Hbar is estimated to be 2.45 μm for the bimodal grain structure in Zr0.50 steel.
evidence_text: Based on Fig. 3, the l_Hbar is estimated to be 2.45 μm.

claim: The combined strengthening contributions from grain boundary strengthening and HDI strengthening are approximately 300 MPa in Zr0.50 steel.
evidence_text: Based on Equations (2)–(4), the strengthening contributions from grain boundary and HDI are ~300 MPa.

claim: A high density of geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) is generated during elasto-plastic co-deformation of soft CG and hard FG zones, inducing extra HDI hardening and contributing to HDI ductility.
evidence_text: For the heterogeneous grain structure, a high density of GNDs is generated during the elasto-plastic co-deformation, as evidenced in Fig. 5b. As the global strain increasing, the co-deformation of soft CG zones and hard FG zones occurs, which further promotes the compatible deformation and induces additional strain hardening... the density of GNDs is increased in the Zr0.50 steel with heterogeneous grain structures, which in turn assist an extra hardening capability, i.e., HDI hardening effect, and thereby leads to manifestation of HDI ductility.

claim: The critical twinning stress for deformation twinning in Zr0.50 steel is ~1.14 GPa, which is lower than its true ultimate tensile strength, based on an estimated stacking fault energy of ~55 mJ/m².
evidence_text: the SFE of FCC matrix in Zr0.50 steel is estimated to be ~55 mJ/m2 (Supplementary text S1). Thus, the critical twinning stress of the Zr0.50 steel is ~1.14 GPa, which is obviously lower than its true ultimate tensile strength (the inset in Fig. 4a).

claim: The stacking fault energy (SFE) of the FCC matrix in Zr0.50 steel is estimated to be ~55 mJ/m².
evidence_text: In present work, the SFE of FCC matrix in Zr0.50 steel is estimated to be ~55 mJ/ m2 (Supplementary text S1).

claim: The critical twinning stress of the Zr0.50 steel is ~1.14 GPa, which is lower than its true ultimate tensile strength.
evidence_text: Thus, the critical twinning stress of the Zr0.50 steel is ~1.14 GPa, which is obviously lower than its true ultimate tensile strength (the inset in Fig. 4a).

claim: The single-phase heterogeneous grain structure contains coarse grains with ~5 μm in size and 65 % area fraction, and fine grains with ~1 μm in size and 35 % area fraction.
evidence_text: a single-phase heterogeneous grain structure, containing coarse grains with ~5 μm in size and 65 % in area and fine grains with ~1 μm in size and 35 % in area
